data.title: Energy Up Alongside Natural Gas Futures Ahead of Storm - Energy Roundup
data.description:
Shares of energy companies rose alongside natural-gas futures ahead of a winter storm.

A historic surge in natural gas futures continued as Winter Storm Fern loomed, threatening to bring heavy snow and plunging temperatures to millions of Americans.

Natural gas for February delivery rose 3.5% to top $5 per million British thermal units. Oil futures fell on easing geopolitical tensions and a reported buildup in U.S. crude stockpiles.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

01-22-26 1712ET

data.title: Trump: Didn't get the impression that judges were skeptical of power to remove Cook
US to complete transfer of Islamic State detainees from Syria to Iraq in coming days, official says
data.description:
WASHINGTON, Jan 22 (Reuters) - The U.S. military expects to complete the transfer of up to 7,000 Islamic State detainees from Syrian prisons to Iraq in the coming days, a U.S. official told Reuters on Thursday, saying hundreds would be moving across the border daily.

The U.S. military's Central Command said on Wednesday its forces had transferred 150 Islamic State detainees from a detention facility in Syria's Hasaka province to Iraq, citing the need to guard against any prison breakout.

The transfers are taking place after the rapid collapse of Kurdish-led forces in northeast Syria triggered concerns over prison security following Tuesday's escape of roughly 200 low-level Islamic State fighters from Syria's Shaddadi prison. Syrian government forces recaptured many of them.

The U.S. official, who spoke on condition of anonymity, said the U.S. transfers prioritized "the most dangerous" Islamic State fighters and noted that they were citizens of many different countries, including in Europe.

Iraqi legal sources said the IS detainees sent from Syria so far include a mix of nationalities, with Iraqis making up the largest group, alongside Arab fighters from other countries and nationals of Britain, Belgium, France, Germany and Sweden.

U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio on Thursday applauded Iraq for taking the prisoners.

"Non-Iraqi terrorists will be in Iraq temporarily; the United States urges countries to take responsibility and repatriate their citizens in these facilities to face justice," Rubio said in a statement.

Islamic State emerged in Iraq and Syria, and at the height of its power from 2014-2017 held swathes of the two countries, ruling over millions of people. Its "caliphate" eventually collapsed after military campaigns by regional governments and a U.S.-led coalition.

The prisons that the Islamic State members are being transferred from are located in northeast Syria's Hasaka province, where the Syrian Democratic Forces had been working closely with the United States for years to secure them.

The U.S. had been a backer of the SDF since 2015, when the force was conceived to expel the Islamic State group from Syria's northeast. The SDF later used that territory to establish an autonomous enclave, with separate civilian and military institutions.

But in late 2024, President Ahmed al-Sharaa's rebel forces ousted longtime ruler Bashar al-Assad and pledged to bring all of Syria under the new government's control, including SDF-run areas.

The Syrian government has recently seized swathes of northern and eastern Syria from Kurdish forces, consolidating Sharaa's rule, in a rapid turn of events that has brought almost all of Syria back under the authority of the Damascus-based state.

The United States has about 1,000 troops in Syria but has long mulled exiting.

With Assad toppled, U.S. President Donald Trump has developed close ties to Sharaa, lifting sanctions and welcoming Syria into an international anti-Islamic State coalition.

U.S. envoy Tom Barrack said on Tuesday the original purpose of the SDF had largely expired, and that the U.S. had no long-term interest in retaining its presence in Syria.

(Reporting by Phil Stewart and Idrees Ali; additional reporting by Maya Gebeily; editing by Diane Craft)

Cathie Wood's 'Big Ideas' for 2026: Tech, Tesla and Deregulation
data.description:
Ark Invest CEO and CIO Cathie Wood discusses her "Big Ideas 2026" report. Speaking with Carol Massar and Tim Stenovec, Wood says a "technology revolution" could lead to an average of more than 7% real GDP growth by the end of the decade. She also says she expects an "entrepreneurial explosion" in the US and discusses her outlook for Tesla (TSLA) and views on deregulation. Cathie Wood's 'Big Ideas': Tech, Tesla and Deregulation

data.title: Csx Corp. announces fourth quarter and full year 2025 results
Capital One Financial Posts Higher 4Q Profit As Lending Increases
data.description:
By Elias Schisgall


Capital One Financial reported a higher profit in the fourth quarter as credit-card and consumer lending trended upwards.

The credit-card company Thursday reported a profit of $2.13 billion, or $3.26 a share, compared with $1.1 billion, or $2.67 a share, in the year-ago quarter.

Stripping out certain one-time items, including charges from Capital One's acquisition of Discover, earnings were $3.86 a share, missing Wall Street's expectation of $4.14 a share, according to FactSet.

The company reported revenue of $15.58 billion, up from $10.19 billion a year earlier. Analysts were expecting $15.47 billion in revenue.

Provision for credit losses increased to $4.14 billion, the company said.

The company said credit-card period-end loans rose 3% to $279.6 billion in the quarter, while consumer banking loans increased 2% to $84.8 billion.

Delinquency rates for credit cards and consumer banking each fell slightly year over year, to 3.93% and 5.17%, respectively.

Capital One said Thursday it plans to acquire Brex, a financial technology company specializing in corporate credit cards, for $5.15 billion in a cash and stock deal. The Brex deal marks another major buy for Capital One after it completed the acquisition Discover Financial for $35.3 billion last year.

The Discover acquisition incurred pre-tax charges in the fourth quarter of about $898 million, or $1.07 a share, the company said.


Write to Elias Schisgall at elias.schisgall@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

01-22-26 1655ET

Honeywell Names CFO, Business Unit Leaders for Aerospace Spinoff
data.description:
By Kelly Cloonan


Honeywell appointed a finance chief and three business unit leaders for its planned aerospace spinoff.

The industrial conglomerate on Thursday named Josh Jepsen as Honeywell Aerospace's chief financial officer, effective Feb. 23. Jepsen currently holds the same role at Deere, where he previously held senior positions across finance, investor relations and accounting.

Honeywell also named three longtime employees as the chief executive officers of the spinoff's business units. The trio will assume their new titles once the spinoff is completed, and will report directly to Honeywell Aerospace's CEO Jim Currier.

Bob Buddecke will lead Honeywell Aerospace's electronic solutions. Buddecke has worked at Honeywell for more than 27 years in roles focused on operations, supply chain, strategic growth and cross-business innovation.

Dave Marinick will lead engines and power systems. Marinick has worked at Honeywell for more than 37 years, and has expertise in business strategy, engineering and large-scale program management, the company said.

Rich DeGraff will lead control systems. DeGraff has held senior roles outside of the company, and has spent 17 years at Honeywell, focusing on customer engagement and business growth.

The spinoff is planned for the second half of 2026. It is expected to be an independent, publicly traded company headquartered in Phoenix.


Write to Kelly Cloonan at kelly.cloonan@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

01-22-26 1650ET
